response.answer:
Section 420 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property. It states that whoever cheats and thereby dishonestly induces the person deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to make, alter or destroy the whole or any part of a valuable security, or to give any person any property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

Section 420 IPC reads as follows:
"Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property. — Whoever cheats and thereby dishonestly induces the person deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to make, alter or destroy the whole or any part of a valuable security, or to give any person any property, or anything signed or sealed which is capable of being converted into a valuable security, with the intention of causing it to be delivered to any person, or of causing the person to whom it is delivered to part with any property, or to make, alter or destroy the whole or any part of a valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine."

query: What is the punishment for murder under IPC?

response.answer: Under the Indian Penal Code (IPC), the punishment for murder is prescribed in Section 302. According to Section 302, whoever commits murder shall be punished with death or imprisonment for life, and also be liable to fine. (IPC, 1860, Section 302)

query: Has Section 377 IPC been struck down?

response.answer:
Yes, Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) has been partially struck down. 

In the landmark case of Navtej Singh Johar vs. Union of India (2018) 1 SCC 791, the Supreme Court of India decriminalized consensual homosexual acts between adults in private, by reading down Section 377 IPC. The Court held that Section 377 IPC, to the extent it criminalizes consensual sexual acts between adults in private, is unconstitutional and violates the fundamental rights of individuals under Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21 of the Constitution of India.

However, the provision still applies to non-consensual acts, acts involving minors, and acts committed in public.
